title: Sarcoidosis
oneLiner: Multi-system granulomatous disorder, often pulmonary, presenting in 20-40s; Afro-Caribbean > Caucasian; can cause progressive lung fibrosis or sudden cardiac death.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Cardiac sarcoidosis (sudden death, heart block):** unexplained syncope, palpitations, pre-syncope – **single finding: ECG showing new RBBB + left axis deviation (bifascicular block) or sustained VT.** Do NOT attribute to benign causes; risk of complete heart block arrest.
- **Pulmonary embolism (acute dyspnoea in sarcoidosis):** sudden onset pleuritic chest pain, unilateral leg swelling, hypoxia disproportionate to CXR – **single finding: Wells score >4 + hypoxia (SpO2 <92%) – order urgent CTPA.** Steroid use increases thrombotic risk.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** cough (dry or minimally productive), progressive dyspnoea, fatigue, night sweats, arthralgia, erythema nodosum (tender red shins)
- **Key exam findings:**
    - Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy (on CXR or exam – may only be seen on imaging)
    - Skin: erythema nodosum, lupus pernio (violaceous plaques on face)
    - Eyes: uveitis (red, painful, photophobic eye)
    - Chest: fine inspiratory crackles (if pulmonary fibrosis)
    - Hepatosplenomegaly (less common)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Tuberculosis** – unilateral hilar/mediastinal involvement, apical infiltrates, positive IGRA/T-SPOT
- **Lymphoma** – usually unilateral or asymmetrical mediastinal mass, B symptoms (night sweats, fever, weight loss), peripheral lymphadenopathy
- **Fungal infection (histoplasmosis, blastomycosis)** – travel history, cavitary lesions on CXR
- **Berylliosis** – occupational exposure (machining, aerospace)
- **Interstitial lung disease (IPF, HP)** – no hilar lymphadenopathy, different HRCT pattern (reticulation, honeycombing)
- **Splitter:** Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy + erythema nodosum → virtually diagnostic of acute sarcoidosis (Löfgren syndrome); TB and lymphoma almost never cause this combination.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
    - CXR (PA + lateral) – look for bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy (R > L), paratracheal nodes; stages 0-4
    - ECG – mandatory: conduction defects, T-wave inversion, RBBB
    - Spirometry + transfer factor (DLCO) – if able
- **Bloods:**
    - FBC, U&E, LFTs, CRP, ESR
    - Serum ACE (often raised but not diagnostic – high false positives)
    - Serum calcium (corrected) – hypercalcaemia in ~10% due to 1,25(OH)2D overproduction
    - 24-hour urine calcium (if available)
- **Imaging:**
    - First-line: CXR (staging: 0: normal, I: BHL, II: BHL + interstitial, III: interstitial only, IV: fibrosis)
    - Second-line: HRCT chest – typical: perilymphatic nodules, bronchovascular thickening, ground-glass; also assess for pulmonary hypertension
- **Special tests:**
    - Bronchoscopy + BAL (lymphocytosis, CD4:CD8 >3.5), transbronchial biopsy (non-caseating granulomas) – gold standard for diagnosis if needed
    - PET-CT (FDG avidity) – can identify active disease but not specific; use if neurological or cardiac involvement suspected
    - Eye slit-lamp exam (if ocular symptoms)
- **Diagnostic criteria:** No single accepted criteria; diagnosis based on compatible clinical/radiographic features + histology (if atypical) + exclusion of other causes. Löfgren syndrome (BHL + erythema nodosum + fever/arthralgia) can be diagnosed clinically.
- **Never-miss test:** **ECG** – one missed cardiac sarcoid can be fatal. If any concerning features (syncope, palpitations, family history of sudden death), proceed to 24-hour Holter, echo, and cardiac MRI.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
    - If acute respiratory failure (type 1 or 2) suspected due to sarcoidosis: start high-dose methylprednisolone 1 g IV after excluding infection (check CRP, procalcitonin, blood cultures). Early senior review.
    - If cardiac sarcoid suspected with unstable arrhythmia: cardiac monitoring, amiodarone loading (avoid if heart block), contact cardiology.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
    - Start prednisolone 0.5-1 mg/kg/day (max 60 mg) orally for 2-4 weeks, then taper slowly (reduce by 5-10 mg every 2-4 weeks based on response). Typical taper over 6-12 months.
    - Consider methotrexate or azathioprine if high risk of relapse or need for prolonged steroids (discuss with respiratory team).
    - VTE prophylaxis (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) if steroid use + immobility + other risk factors.
    - Bone protection: calcium + vitamin D (if no pre-existing hypercalcaemia – check corrected Ca first). If hypercalcaemia present, withhold supplements and consider bisphosphonates.
    - Gastric protection: PPIs (e.g., omeprazole 20 mg daily) if steroids.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
    - Taper steroids under respiratory clinic follow-up
    - Step-up therapy if inadequate response: methotrexate (7.5-25 mg once weekly), azathioprine (1-2 mg/kg/day), or anti-TNF (infliximab 3-5 mg/kg IV) — reserve for refractory pulmonary, cardiac, or neurosarcoidosis.
    - Monitor: FBC, LFTs, U&E, calcium, spirometry at each clinic visit (every 3-6 months). Annual eye exam if on hydroxychloroquine.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
    - Fluids: no specific fluids required – maintenance IV if NBM. Avoid vitamin D-containing fluids.
    - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (check weight, renal function)
    - Diet: general, no restrictions unless hypercalcaemia (then avoid high calcium + vitamin D foods)
    - Observations: routine NEWS2 4-hourly; if on high-dose steroids → consider BP monitoring + BM if diabetic.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line: Prednisolone** – oral, start 0.5-1 mg/kg/day (max 60 mg) × 2-4 weeks, then taper. Key: never stop abruptly – risk adrenal crisis.
- **Common alternatives:**
    - Methotrexate: 7.5-25 mg once weekly (oral or SC) + folic acid 5 mg weekly (except on methotrexate day). Check LFTs, FBC, renal every month initially.
    - Azathioprine: 1-2 mg/kg/day oral. Check TPMT before starting (risk of myelotoxicity if deficient).
    - Hydroxychloroquine: 200-400 mg daily (skin and joint predominant). Baseline eye exam; annual retinal screening.
    - Infliximab: 3-5 mg/kg IV at weeks 0, 2, 6 then q8w. Contraindicated in TB – screen with IGRA + CXR first.
- **Key contraindications/interactions:** Live vaccines (MMR, yellow fever) contra – avoid on immunosuppression. Methotrexate + NSAIDs → increased nephrotoxicity. Azathioprine + allopurinol → severe myelosuppression (reduce azathioprine to 25% if allopurinol needed).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
    - Methotrexate: avoid if eGFR <30; dose reduce if LFTs >2x ULN.
    - Azathioprine: adjust dose if severe hepatic impairment.
    - Infliximab: caution if hepatitis (check HBsAg, HCV).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Steroids – abrupt discontinuation of high-dose prednisolone causing adrenal crisis.** Always reduce gradually (e.g., reduce by 5 mg every 2-4 weeks once disease controlled). Check AM cortisol (>500 nmol/L) before total cessation if on >5 mg for >3 weeks.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
    - Hypercalcaemia (worsens with sun exposure/vitamin D)
    - Arrhythmias (heart block, ventricular tachycardia) – cardiac sarcoid
    - Uveitis (can cause permanent vision loss if untreated)
    - Erythema nodosum (usually self-limiting)
- **Chronic:**
    - Progressive pulmonary fibrosis ± pulmonary hypertension
    - Chronic uveitis, glaucoma, cataracts
    - Neurosarcoidosis (cranial nerve palsies, aseptic meningitis, seizures)
    - Cardiac sarcoid (dilated cardiomyopathy, restrictive pattern)
    - Disfiguring skin: lupus pernio (requires dermatology input)
sections.Prognosis: - 60-80% spontaneous remission within 1-2 years (especially Löfgren syndrome). 10-20% develop chronic progressive disease, mainly pulmonary fibrosis. Mortality ~1-5% from respiratory failure or cardiac involvement.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Do an ECG on every sarcoidosis patient** — ask about syncope/palpitations. If any cardiac suspicion, order 24-h Holter, echo, and discuss cardiology MRI.
- **If you see erythema nodosum + bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, you can diagnose Löfgren syndrome clinically** — no need for biopsy. Start steroids if symptomatic (fever, arthralgia).
- **Check serum calcium at diagnosis and every follow-up** — hypercalcaemia is treatable and can cause renal impairment. Advise sun avoidance and no unmonitored vitamin D supplements.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not start steroids in asymptomatic stage I (BHL only)** — many resolve spontaneously. Only treat if symptoms (cough, dyspnoea) or progressive disease (falling PFTs, worsening HRCT).
- **Do not give over-the-counter vitamin D or calcium supplements** unless prescribed by the team — can precipitate severe hypercalcaemia.
- **Before starting anti-TNF therapy (infliximab), always screen for TB** with IGRA (T-SPOT) and CXR. Activates latent TB in up to 5% of cases.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
    - New shortness of breath or worsening of existing symptoms
    - Chest pain, palpitations, or fainting (syncope)
    - Vision changes (blurring, redness, pain) – urgent eye review needed
    - Severe headache, neck stiffness, or seizures (possible neurosarcoid)
- **Follow-up:** Respiratory clinic within 4 weeks; if started on steroids, taper plan provided. If on methotrexate/azathioprine, blood monitoring arranged (FBC, LFTs, U&E monthly). Eye clinic if uveitis.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (respiratory reg or consultant) if:**
    - NEWS2 ≥5
    - Lactate >2 mmol/L
    - GCS <13 or new focal neurology
    - New arrhythmia (especially heart block or VT) on ECG
    - Rapidly worsening hypoxia (SpO2 <90% on 15L O2)
- **Call crash team (2222) if:**
    - Cardiac arrest or severe bradycardia due to heart block (apply pacing pads)
    - Seizure (suspect neurosarcoid – treat with benzodiazepines per protocol)
    - Respiratory arrest or severe type 2 respiratory failure (consider non-invasive ventilation)

rawMarkdown:
## Sarcoidosis
**One-liner:** Multi-system granulomatous disorder, often pulmonary, presenting in 20-40s; Afro-Caribbean > Caucasian; can cause progressive lung fibrosis or sudden cardiac death.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Cardiac sarcoidosis (sudden death, heart block):** unexplained syncope, palpitations, pre-syncope – **single finding: ECG showing new RBBB + left axis deviation (bifascicular block) or sustained VT.** Do NOT attribute to benign causes; risk of complete heart block arrest.
- **Pulmonary embolism (acute dyspnoea in sarcoidosis):** sudden onset pleuritic chest pain, unilateral leg swelling, hypoxia disproportionate to CXR – **single finding: Wells score >4 + hypoxia (SpO2 <92%) – order urgent CTPA.** Steroid use increases thrombotic risk.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** cough (dry or minimally productive), progressive dyspnoea, fatigue, night sweats, arthralgia, erythema nodosum (tender red shins)
- **Key exam findings:**
    - Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy (on CXR or exam – may only be seen on imaging)
    - Skin: erythema nodosum, lupus pernio (violaceous plaques on face)
    - Eyes: uveitis (red, painful, photophobic eye)
    - Chest: fine inspiratory crackles (if pulmonary fibrosis)
    - Hepatosplenomegaly (less common)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Tuberculosis** – unilateral hilar/mediastinal involvement, apical infiltrates, positive IGRA/T-SPOT
- **Lymphoma** – usually unilateral or asymmetrical mediastinal mass, B symptoms (night sweats, fever, weight loss), peripheral lymphadenopathy
- **Fungal infection (histoplasmosis, blastomycosis)** – travel history, cavitary lesions on CXR
- **Berylliosis** – occupational exposure (machining, aerospace)
- **Interstitial lung disease (IPF, HP)** – no hilar lymphadenopathy, different HRCT pattern (reticulation, honeycombing)
- **Splitter:** Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy + erythema nodosum → virtually diagnostic of acute sarcoidosis (Löfgren syndrome); TB and lymphoma almost never cause this combination.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
    - CXR (PA + lateral) – look for bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy (R > L), paratracheal nodes; stages 0-4
    - ECG – mandatory: conduction defects, T-wave inversion, RBBB
    - Spirometry + transfer factor (DLCO) – if able
- **Bloods:**
    - FBC, U&E, LFTs, CRP, ESR
    - Serum ACE (often raised but not diagnostic – high false positives)
    - Serum calcium (corrected) – hypercalcaemia in ~10% due to 1,25(OH)2D overproduction
    - 24-hour urine calcium (if available)
- **Imaging:**
    - First-line: CXR (staging: 0: normal, I: BHL, II: BHL + interstitial, III: interstitial only, IV: fibrosis)
    - Second-line: HRCT chest – typical: perilymphatic nodules, bronchovascular thickening, ground-glass; also assess for pulmonary hypertension
- **Special tests:**
    - Bronchoscopy + BAL (lymphocytosis, CD4:CD8 >3.5), transbronchial biopsy (non-caseating granulomas) – gold standard for diagnosis if needed
    - PET-CT (FDG avidity) – can identify active disease but not specific; use if neurological or cardiac involvement suspected
    - Eye slit-lamp exam (if ocular symptoms)
- **Diagnostic criteria:** No single accepted criteria; diagnosis based on compatible clinical/radiographic features + histology (if atypical) + exclusion of other causes. Löfgren syndrome (BHL + erythema nodosum + fever/arthralgia) can be diagnosed clinically.
- **Never-miss test:** **ECG** – one missed cardiac sarcoid can be fatal. If any concerning features (syncope, palpitations, family history of sudden death), proceed to 24-hour Holter, echo, and cardiac MRI.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
    - If acute respiratory failure (type 1 or 2) suspected due to sarcoidosis: start high-dose methylprednisolone 1 g IV after excluding infection (check CRP, procalcitonin, blood cultures). Early senior review.
    - If cardiac sarcoid suspected with unstable arrhythmia: cardiac monitoring, amiodarone loading (avoid if heart block), contact cardiology.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
    - Start prednisolone 0.5-1 mg/kg/day (max 60 mg) orally for 2-4 weeks, then taper slowly (reduce by 5-10 mg every 2-4 weeks based on response). Typical taper over 6-12 months.
    - Consider methotrexate or azathioprine if high risk of relapse or need for prolonged steroids (discuss with respiratory team).
    - VTE prophylaxis (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) if steroid use + immobility + other risk factors.
    - Bone protection: calcium + vitamin D (if no pre-existing hypercalcaemia – check corrected Ca first). If hypercalcaemia present, withhold supplements and consider bisphosphonates.
    - Gastric protection: PPIs (e.g., omeprazole 20 mg daily) if steroids.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
    - Taper steroids under respiratory clinic follow-up
    - Step-up therapy if inadequate response: methotrexate (7.5-25 mg once weekly), azathioprine (1-2 mg/kg/day), or anti-TNF (infliximab 3-5 mg/kg IV) — reserve for refractory pulmonary, cardiac, or neurosarcoidosis.
    - Monitor: FBC, LFTs, U&E, calcium, spirometry at each clinic visit (every 3-6 months). Annual eye exam if on hydroxychloroquine.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
    - Fluids: no specific fluids required – maintenance IV if NBM. Avoid vitamin D-containing fluids.
    - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (check weight, renal function)
    - Diet: general, no restrictions unless hypercalcaemia (then avoid high calcium + vitamin D foods)
    - Observations: routine NEWS2 4-hourly; if on high-dose steroids → consider BP monitoring + BM if diabetic.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line: Prednisolone** – oral, start 0.5-1 mg/kg/day (max 60 mg) × 2-4 weeks, then taper. Key: never stop abruptly – risk adrenal crisis.
- **Common alternatives:**
    - Methotrexate: 7.5-25 mg once weekly (oral or SC) + folic acid 5 mg weekly (except on methotrexate day). Check LFTs, FBC, renal every month initially.
    - Azathioprine: 1-2 mg/kg/day oral. Check TPMT before starting (risk of myelotoxicity if deficient).
    - Hydroxychloroquine: 200-400 mg daily (skin and joint predominant). Baseline eye exam; annual retinal screening.
    - Infliximab: 3-5 mg/kg IV at weeks 0, 2, 6 then q8w. Contraindicated in TB – screen with IGRA + CXR first.
- **Key contraindications/interactions:** Live vaccines (MMR, yellow fever) contra – avoid on immunosuppression. Methotrexate + NSAIDs → increased nephrotoxicity. Azathioprine + allopurinol → severe myelosuppression (reduce azathioprine to 25% if allopurinol needed).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
    - Methotrexate: avoid if eGFR <30; dose reduce if LFTs >2x ULN.
    - Azathioprine: adjust dose if severe hepatic impairment.
    - Infliximab: caution if hepatitis (check HBsAg, HCV).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Steroids – abrupt discontinuation of high-dose prednisolone causing adrenal crisis.** Always reduce gradually (e.g., reduce by 5 mg every 2-4 weeks once disease controlled). Check AM cortisol (>500 nmol/L) before total cessation if on >5 mg for >3 weeks.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
    - Hypercalcaemia (worsens with sun exposure/vitamin D)
    - Arrhythmias (heart block, ventricular tachycardia) – cardiac sarcoid
    - Uveitis (can cause permanent vision loss if untreated)
    - Erythema nodosum (usually self-limiting)
- **Chronic:**
    - Progressive pulmonary fibrosis ± pulmonary hypertension
    - Chronic uveitis, glaucoma, cataracts
    - Neurosarcoidosis (cranial nerve palsies, aseptic meningitis, seizures)
    - Cardiac sarcoid (dilated cardiomyopathy, restrictive pattern)
    - Disfiguring skin: lupus pernio (requires dermatology input)

### Prognosis
- 60-80% spontaneous remission within 1-2 years (especially Löfgren syndrome). 10-20% develop chronic progressive disease, mainly pulmonary fibrosis. Mortality ~1-5% from respiratory failure or cardiac involvement.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Do an ECG on every sarcoidosis patient** — ask about syncope/palpitations. If any cardiac suspicion, order 24-h Holter, echo, and discuss cardiology MRI.
- **If you see erythema nodosum + bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, you can diagnose Löfgren syndrome clinically** — no need for biopsy. Start steroids if symptomatic (fever, arthralgia).
- **Check serum calcium at diagnosis and every follow-up** — hypercalcaemia is treatable and can cause renal impairment. Advise sun avoidance and no unmonitored vitamin D supplements.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not start steroids in asymptomatic stage I (BHL only)** — many resolve spontaneously. Only treat if symptoms (cough, dyspnoea) or progressive disease (falling PFTs, worsening HRCT).
- **Do not give over-the-counter vitamin D or calcium supplements** unless prescribed by the team — can precipitate severe hypercalcaemia.
- **Before starting anti-TNF therapy (infliximab), always screen for TB** with IGRA (T-SPOT) and CXR. Activates latent TB in up to 5% of cases.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
    - New shortness of breath or worsening of existing symptoms
    - Chest pain, palpitations, or fainting (syncope)
    - Vision changes (blurring, redness, pain) – urgent eye review needed
    - Severe headache, neck stiffness, or seizures (possible neurosarcoid)
- **Follow-up:** Respiratory clinic within 4 weeks; if started on steroids, taper plan provided. If on methotrexate/azathioprine, blood monitoring arranged (FBC, LFTs, U&E monthly). Eye clinic if uveitis.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (respiratory reg or consultant) if:**
    - NEWS2 ≥5
    - Lactate >2 mmol/L
    - GCS <13 or new focal neurology
    - New arrhythmia (especially heart block or VT) on ECG
    - Rapidly worsening hypoxia (SpO2 <90% on 15L O2)
- **Call crash team (2222) if:**
    - Cardiac arrest or severe bradycardia due to heart block (apply pacing pads)
    - Seizure (suspect neurosarcoid – treat with benzodiazepines per protocol)
    - Respiratory arrest or severe type 2 respiratory failure (consider non-invasive ventilation)
